game_name: Super Street Fighter II: The New Challengers

danger_signals.visual_form: Beat-up character portraits with visible injury marks and blood (arcade/international versions)
danger_signals.meaning: Opponent health depletion; visual indicator of combat damage accumulation

danger_signals.visual_form: Destructible/dynamic stage elements and themed environmental backdrops
danger_signals.meaning: Stage-specific danger contextualizes the fight location geographically

danger_signals.visual_form: Flashing visual effects, impact particles, and character animation states during Hadouken, Shoryuken, etc.
danger_signals.meaning: High-damage attack incoming; opponent vulnerability window or counter-opportunity

safety_signals.visual_form: Horizontal bar at top of screen representing remaining health per fighter
safety_signals.meaning: Quantifies survivor state; full bar = safe, depleted bar = danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Character freeze-frame with distinct pose; announcer voice confirmation
safety_signals.meaning: Match state transitions; signals safe zone between rounds or match completion

safety_signals.visual_form: Character standing upright at mid-screen distance with defensive posture
safety_signals.meaning: Neither combatant actively threatening; temporary combat de-escalation

faction_indicators.color_identity: Purple/magenta associated with M. Bison's special effects and stage theming
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: M. Bison emblem; Cammy's Shadaloo connection (character tie)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Cammy introduced as Shadaloo agent with mysterious past; T. Hawk's ancestral conflict against Shadaloo land seizure
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Stage designs reflect opposing nations and territories being defended/contested

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue (Ryu), red/white (Ken) color schemes; traditional dojo aesthetics
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Hadouken projectile; Japanese-themed stage backgrounds; dojo architecture
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Ryu and Ken represent classical Street Fighter lineage; Fei Long (Hong Kong) represents Asian cinema martial arts crossover
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Japanese temple, dojo, and training ground stage designs

faction_indicators.color_identity: Cammy (blonde, UK), T. Hawk (Native American), Dee Jay (Jamaican), Guile (American military)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: National/cultural clothing (Cammy's SAS-style outfit, T. Hawk's tribal regalia, Dee Jay's reggae aesthetics)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four new challengers expand global representation; each carries distinct cultural visual markers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: International stage settings: British, Mexican, Jamaican, Hong Kong backdrops reflect character origins

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: HUD updates showing current opponent tier; transition from random eight opponents to four Grand Masters (Balrog, Vega, Sagat, M. Bison)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI opponent difficulty scaling; Grand Master characters employ refined special techniques (M. Bison's Devil Reverse, Zangief's Atomic Buster) signaling escalation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: New character special moves displayed through animation and effect particles (Ryu's Fire Hadouken, Ken's flaming Shoryuken)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All twelve World Warriors receive balanced move refinements; new characters introduce distinct move-sets signaling meta-evolution

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage environments reflect contested regions: T. Hawk's Mexico stage represents ancestral homeland dispossession; international fighter recruitment across stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Character roster expansion to four new challengers signals world recruitment for tournament; Cammy's Shadaloo tie manifests ongoing organizational conflict

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: SNES version removes blood effects and beat-up damage portraiture due to Nintendo policy; regional music instrument sampling differences (synthesized US brasses vs. realistic JP brasses)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Announcer voice integration differs by region; US version adds character name callouts absent in Japanese/European versions

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Arcade vs. Home console hardware capability
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: CP System II arcade original features 'more sophisticated graphics and audio'; SNES adaptation uses 32 megabit cartridge with XBAND network support
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: HUD and stage graphics completely remade for SNES port; new opening sequence featuring Ryu's Hadouken replaces original generic crowd fight sequence

environmental_storytelling.example: Cammy's stage and character design communicate British SAS special forces background with mysterious Shadaloo connection; Dee Jay's Jamaican music-obsessed identity embedded in character portrait and stage theming; T. Hawk's indigenous/ceremonial visual design contextualizes ancestral homeland dispossession narrative

Stages visually represent distinct world locations tied to character nationalities: Japanese dojo for Ryu/Ken tradition, Hong Kong cinema-inspired setting for Fei Long, temple/religious structures for certain fighters, signaling global tournament dispersal

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character costume and color scheme customization
environmental_storytelling.example: Eight selectable color schemes per character (original, Champion Edition, Hyper Fighting variants, plus five new schemes) allow visual expression of character iteration history and player identity investment

environmental_storytelling.technique: Visual damage accumulation in portrait frames
environmental_storytelling.example: Beat-up character portraits showing progressive injury marks and blood (removed in SNES due to censorship) communicate round-by-round attrition and narrative dominance shifts
Ryu launching Hadouken toward screen replaces generic crowd fight opening—directly communicates series protagonist identity and signature technique as world narrative focus

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Executes refined special techniques including new Devil Reverse move; employs most sophisticated AI patterns; controls Shadaloo faction presence across narrative
Character design communicates SAS operative identity with mysterious Shadaloo past; visual tie to M. Bison suggests organizational entanglement and potential alliance/conflict complexity
Indigenous visual identity and gameplay moves communicate reclamation narrative; behavioral pattern vs. Shadaloo opponents suggests territorial conflict motivation

Hong Kong cinema aesthetic combined with Bruce Lee homage communicates entertainment-to-martial-arts crossover identity; non-faction narrative focuses on personal validation rather than geopolitical conflict
Jamaican reggae-influenced character design and music theme communicate cultural artist identity; tournament participation motivated by creative inspiration rather than factional loyalty
New Fire Hadouken (Shakunetsu Hadouken) technique signals power progression; opening sequence focus communicates narrative centrality; participates in global tournament as wandering martial artist
Appear only after defeating eight random opponents; refined special techniques and advanced AI signify championship-tier threat level; represent tournament elite cohort with M. Bison

notes: Super Street Fighter II communicates world-state primarily through character roster expansion, international stage diversity, and narrative faction presence (Shadaloo vs. independent/allied fighters). Danger is signaled through damage portraiture, special move effects, and opponent tier escalation. The game's manifestation layer relies on visual character differentiation (costume, color, cultural markers) rather than explicit UI narrative elements. Regional censorship differences (blood removal, music instrumentation variance) communicate platform-specific world-state constraints. The new opening cinematic focusing on Ryu establishes protagonist narrative centrality for this iteration. Fighting-game paradigm limits explicit world-state communication—most worldbuilding occurs through character flavor, stage selection, and tournament structure rather than environmental hazards or dynamic world conditions.
